root_text.an9.36:1.1: “Paṭhamampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.2: dutiyampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.3: tatiyampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.4: catutthampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.5: ākāsānañcāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.6: viññāṇañcāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.7: ākiñcaññāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.8: nevasaññānāsaññāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi;
root_text.an9.36:1.9: saññāvedayitanirodhampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmi.
root_text.an9.36:2.1: ‘Paṭhamampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:2.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi …pe… paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:2.4: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
root_text.an9.36:2.6: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
root_text.an9.36:2.7: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
root_text.an9.36:2.8: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
root_text.an9.36:2.9: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
root_text.an9.36:3.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
root_text.an9.36:3.2: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi …pe… paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:3.3: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
root_text.an9.36:3.5: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
root_text.an9.36:3.6: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
root_text.an9.36:3.7: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
root_text.an9.36:3.8: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
root_text.an9.36:3.9: ‘Paṭhamampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti,
root_text.an9.36:3.10: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:4.3: ‘catutthampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:4.5: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā pubbeva somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā adukkhamasukhaṁ upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:4.6: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
root_text.an9.36:4.8: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
root_text.an9.36:4.9: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
root_text.an9.36:4.10: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
root_text.an9.36:4.11: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
root_text.an9.36:5.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
root_text.an9.36:5.2: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sukhassa ca pahānā …pe… catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:5.3: So yadeva tattha hoti rūpagataṁ vedanāgataṁ …pe…
root_text.an9.36:5.5: ‘Catutthampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:6.1: ‘Ākāsānañcāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, jhānaṁ nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:6.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā paṭighasaññānaṁ atthaṅgamā nānattasaññānaṁ amanasikārā ‘ananto ākāso’ti ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:6.4: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
root_text.an9.36:6.6: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
root_text.an9.36:6.7: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
root_text.an9.36:6.8: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
root_text.an9.36:6.9: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
root_text.an9.36:7.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
root_text.an9.36:7.2: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso rūpasaññānaṁ samatikkamā paṭighasaññānaṁ atthaṅgamā nānattasaññānaṁ amanasikārā ‘ananto ākāso’ti ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:7.3: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ …pe…
root_text.an9.36:7.6: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:8.2: ākiñcaññāyatanampāhaṁ, bhikkhave, nissāya āsavānaṁ khayaṁ vadāmī’ti, iti kho panetaṁ vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:8.4: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:8.5: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
root_text.an9.36:8.7: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
root_text.an9.36:8.8: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
root_text.an9.36:8.9: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
root_text.an9.36:8.10: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
root_text.an9.36:9.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, issāso vā issāsantevāsī vā tiṇapurisarūpake vā mattikāpuñje vā yoggaṁ karitvā, so aparena samayena dūrepātī ca hoti akkhaṇavedhī ca mahato ca kāyassa padāletā;
root_text.an9.36:9.2: evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sabbaso viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ samatikkamma ‘natthi kiñcī’ti ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an9.36:9.3: So yadeva tattha hoti vedanāgataṁ saññāgataṁ saṅkhāragataṁ viññāṇagataṁ, te dhamme aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato samanupassati.
root_text.an9.36:9.5: So tehi dhammehi cittaṁ paṭivāpetvā amatāya dhātuyā cittaṁ upasaṁharati:
root_text.an9.36:9.6: ‘etaṁ santaṁ etaṁ paṇītaṁ yadidaṁ sabbasaṅkhārasamatho sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo taṇhākkhayo virāgo nirodho nibbānan’ti.
root_text.an9.36:9.7: So tattha ṭhito āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti.
root_text.an9.36:9.8: No ce āsavānaṁ khayaṁ pāpuṇāti, teneva dhammarāgena tāya dhammanandiyā pañcannaṁ orambhāgiyānaṁ saṁyojanānaṁ parikkhayā opapātiko hoti tattha parinibbāyī anāvattidhammo tasmā lokā.
root_text.an9.36:9.10: iti yaṁ taṁ vuttaṁ, idametaṁ paṭicca vuttaṁ.
root_text.an9.36:10.1: Iti kho, bhikkhave, yāvatā saññāsamāpatti tāvatā aññāpaṭivedho.
root_text.an9.36:10.2: Yāni ca kho imāni, bhikkhave, nissāya dve āyatanāni—
root_text.an9.36:10.3: nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasamāpatti ca saññāvedayitanirodho ca, jhāyīhete, bhikkhave, samāpattikusalehi samāpattivuṭṭhānakusalehi samāpajjitvā vuṭṭhahitvā sammā akkhātabbānīti vadāmī”ti.

translation_text.an9.36:1.1: “Mendicants, I say that the first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.2: The second absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.3: The third absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.4: The fourth absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.5: The dimension of infinite space is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.6: The dimension of infinite consciousness is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.7: The dimension of nothingness is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.8: The dimension of neither perception nor non-perception is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:1.9: The cessation of perception and feeling is also a basis for ending the defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:2.1: ‘The first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.’
translation_text.an9.36:2.2: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an9.36:2.3: Take a mendicant who, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption.
translation_text.an9.36:2.4: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.an9.36:2.5: They turn their mind away from those things,
translation_text.an9.36:2.6: and apply it to freedom from death:
translation_text.an9.36:2.7: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’
translation_text.an9.36:2.8: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:2.9: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.
translation_text.an9.36:3.1: It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects.
translation_text.an9.36:3.2: In the same way a noble disciple, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, enters and remains in the first absorption.
translation_text.an9.36:3.3: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.an9.36:3.4: They turn their mind away from those things,
translation_text.an9.36:3.5: and apply it to freedom from death:
translation_text.an9.36:3.6: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’
translation_text.an9.36:3.7: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:3.8: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.
translation_text.an9.36:3.9: ‘The first absorption is a basis for ending the defilements.’
translation_text.an9.36:3.10: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an9.36:4.1: ‘The second absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ …
translation_text.an9.36:4.2: ‘The third absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ …
translation_text.an9.36:4.3: ‘The fourth absorption is also a basis for ending the defilements.’ …
translation_text.an9.36:6.1: ‘The dimension of infinite space is also a basis for ending the defilements.’
translation_text.an9.36:6.2: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an9.36:6.3: Take a mendicant who, going totally beyond perceptions of form, with the disappearance of perceptions of impingement, not focusing on perceptions of diversity, aware that ‘space is infinite’, enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space.
translation_text.an9.36:6.4: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.an9.36:6.5: They turn their mind away from those things,
translation_text.an9.36:6.6: and apply it to freedom from death:
translation_text.an9.36:6.7: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’
translation_text.an9.36:6.8: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:6.9: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.
translation_text.an9.36:7.1: It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects.
translation_text.an9.36:7.2: In the same way, take a mendicant who enters and remains in the dimension of infinite space. …
translation_text.an9.36:7.5: ‘The dimension of infinite space is a basis for ending the defilements.’
translation_text.an9.36:7.6: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an9.36:8.1: ‘The dimension of infinite consciousness is a basis for ending the defilements.’ …
translation_text.an9.36:8.2: ‘The dimension of nothingness is a basis for ending the defilements.’
translation_text.an9.36:8.3: That’s what I said, but why did I say it?
translation_text.an9.36:8.4: Take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.
translation_text.an9.36:8.5: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.an9.36:8.6: They turn their mind away from those things,
translation_text.an9.36:8.7: and apply it to freedom from death:
translation_text.an9.36:8.8: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’
translation_text.an9.36:8.9: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:8.10: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.
translation_text.an9.36:9.1: It’s like an archer or their apprentice who first practices on a straw man or a clay model. At a later time they become a long-distance shooter, a marksman, who shatters large objects.
translation_text.an9.36:9.2: In the same way, take a mendicant who, going totally beyond the dimension of infinite consciousness, aware that ‘there is nothing at all’, enters and remains in the dimension of nothingness.
translation_text.an9.36:9.3: They contemplate the phenomena there—included in feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness—as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.an9.36:9.4: They turn their mind away from those things,
translation_text.an9.36:9.5: and apply it to freedom from death:
translation_text.an9.36:9.6: ‘This is peaceful; this is sublime—that is, the stilling of all activities, the letting go of all attachments, the ending of craving, fading away, cessation, extinguishment.’
translation_text.an9.36:9.7: Abiding in that they attain the ending of defilements.
translation_text.an9.36:9.8: If they don’t attain the ending of defilements, with the ending of the five lower fetters they’re reborn spontaneously, because of their passion and love for that meditation. They are extinguished there, and are not liable to return from that world.
translation_text.an9.36:9.9: ‘The dimension of nothingness is a basis for ending the defilements.’
translation_text.an9.36:9.10: That’s what I said, and this is why I said it.
translation_text.an9.36:10.1: And so, mendicants, penetration to enlightenment extends as far as attainments with perception.
translation_text.an9.36:10.2: But the two dimensions that depend on these—
translation_text.an9.36:10.3: the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, and the cessation of perception and feeling—are properly explained by meditators who are skilled in these attainments and skilled in emerging from them, after they’ve entered them and emerged from them.”
